reviews.review: One of the all time greats. Nothing more to say. Who hasn't played this yet: what the hell are you waiting for?!
Everything in this game is amazing. It's the best game I've ever played. The laws of physics, the design of the world, the gameplay mechanics, it's all very extraordinary.

I bought this game on black friday 2019 when I was 10, and still now, 7 years later, this is still the best game I've ever played. All the shrines are a joy to play through and feel rewarding to beat. It reinvented the series and feels fresh compared to Skyward Sword. The art style is the best I've ever seen from a Nintendo game and looks absolutely stunning. its one of, if not the best, games of all time, and if my memory got erased, the first thing I'm doing is playing this game again.

a **** masterpiece, My favorite zelda game EVER, and im a big zelda fan. I would give it a 9.9, because of the Thunderblight Ganon.
ChoppedFish out!

The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild has completely redefined one of the greatest video game series of all time. The world is so vast but so full of life. Everything from the story to the characters to the design is absolutely perfect.  So many games promise freedom, but come far too short. Breath of the Wild offers you perfect exploration and choice. Exploration and creativity will always be rewarded. While this game may have some very small issues, they are completely overwhelmed by all the positives. This is a game so perfect you will think about it long after you finish.

This game is perfect. For me, the best game ever made. I like everything in this game. Its exploration, the music, the history and the mechanics are exceptional. It's a game that redefines this saga and all the open-world games. It's also a beautiful game in terms of graphics, even if it's moved by the Nintendo Switch. In conclusion, BotW is a game that you have to play at least once.

Gave it a real go, but overall, extremely boring. I get the point is to explore a post apocalyptic world, but I've never experienced one so empty and mind numbing to traverse. Most of the game is walking or riding from place to place, only to discover the place is not even that fun. The world never gets more complicated or more interesting than the tutorial area. Maybe if the map was smaller it would be better. I love the total freedom and creativity allowed to solve problems, but that just wasn't enough to hold my interest, especially when a lot of the time you couldn't really apply it out in the world. Enemies and combat is repetitive, weapons always breaking kills any feeling of progression and it makes you feel like you can never really use your good weapons in case you don't find something good again. I just kept waiting for the game to actually start if that makes sense, but it never really did.
Completely misses the mark on what a zelda game should and should not be. A good open world snadbox, but a completely terrible zelda game.

This game is amazing, and introduced me to the legend of Zelda series. I loved everything about this game the story, the combat, and even side quests. A few problems like weapon durability and ganon being easy do show up but their so dismissed by the pure amount of amazing things this game did.
This game is so fun and addictive, and there's just so many things to do it's overwhelming at times (in a good way). 10/10

A definitive masterpiece.

The visuals, the music, the simple but masterable combat, the unique and versatile abilities, the pure joy in exploration, the sheer amount of content and things to do on the sandbox map, it all just comes together to make such a great package.

what a beautiful change of pace to an already beautiful series this game feels like Nintendo's final chance before release to show that the switch would be a good console and rectify the mistakes of the Wii u this game plays feels runs like a modern masterpiece and it even bypasses its successor in originality and creativity this game is THE switch game THE Zelda game and a redefinition of what the expectation out of the Zelda franchise will be for  years to come

Fantastic, entertaining, ground-breaking, beautiful. Top-notch design, innovative, great music. Two negative aspects: world can feel empty and story could have been improved. All previous Zelda games haven't had these two issues.

I think with the release of its sequel, Breath of the Wild has taken a bit of a back seat in people's minds. But replaying this, I still find it much more captivating and well rounded than Tears of the Kingdom is. The world is full of life and things to do. This is Nintendo's Skyrim.
BeThe best game I ever played. The open world 10/10 character’s 8/10 items 10/10 the whole game 10/10
Absolutely peak,  just an amazing game with all of its different features. The world and the music fit together amazingly well. Instant top 5 games I've played.

the best open world game you can play, the exploration is reward and you always have something to do when you find a big tower you go there but on the way you find some mobs to kill and then you see another camp and you find a village and at the end of the day  you find out you're at the other side of the map from where you wanted to go first

The gameplay is fluid, satisfying and somewhat challenging. The story is well crafted and beautifully executed. The visuals and graphics are outstanding and the world building is immaculate. The easiest 10/10 I've ever given

Huge fan of the series. Infact link to the past is in my top 5 ever but I just find that one thing totally ruins it. Every 3rd hit your weapon breaks. If you’re against a tough enemy, you spend half the time in the menu changing your weapons. I really hate that feature

If it's your first time playing a Zelda game, then this will dive you into hours of exploration and depth. I love Breath of the Wild, with it's awesome characters, soundtrack, and, most of all, the story. I very much do recommend this game! As well as Tears of the Kingdom.
This is the best game in history. this game have a very big world with a lot of things to do, the history of the game is really good and the characters have personality.

ABSOLUTE VIDEOGAME 900p 30FPS!

Trust me, just play it, it's a beautiful experience!
10/10. I don’t care what the Ocaria of Time fanboys say. This game is amazing.
I wouldn't say its a bad game. It just never clicked with me. Probably just not for me.

The game is overrated. I'm a PC and PS gamer. I don't know what positive things I can say about this game. Exploring the world is satisfying, and magical abilities provide interesting opportunities to show off. That's it for the positives. The graphics aren't the best, the gameplay is repetitive and boring, and the dialogue? There's no dialogue, everything you must read, not even interesting, and the side quests are the same. I wouldn't recommend it to anyone.
Highkey and prob one the best launch titels of a console (we are gonna ignore the fact that this came out on wii u first 🫩)

Peak of game. This is what a game is supposed to be. This game speaks the language of gaming. You can give this game to a little kid, a teenager, a young adult, an old adult and all of them would enjoy it no matter the age because its not just masterpiece of a zelda game and open world but because it could be found extremely enjoyable to someone who has never played a game before. More 10/10 games should be like this, it can literally turn someone who never played games into a gamer. Enjoyed every second of it.
This is one of the greatest games that I have ever played it is better than cory in the house.
My favourite Nintendo Switch game. Just a masterpiecie. Must play for literally everyone

this is an excellent game, with a handful of disappointing issues much like most zelda titles. weapons breaking eventually makes sense after you get into things, but it's not something any zelda fan wanted or expected. the motion control puzzles are consistently broken, climbing in rain is ridiculous (not fixed in totk either), and the inputs sometimes seem to be a bit delayed or missed during combat. still, it's generally a great experience. i could write a 10 page thesis about this game and which aspects i loved or hated, but i wont do that today. most of the popular complaints about the game are irrelevant, because it's easy to recommend.

I could not stand this game. There were some game design choices that added up to where I couldn't play the game anymore.

1. Breakable weapons.
2. Lack of directions.
3. Motion control puzzles.
4. Not having a recipe book, for recipes you have already discovered.
5. Having to change out of metal armour during in-game storm. I finally gave up after that.

Genuinely 10/10 game, stared playing when I was 4 and 9 years later still play it, and I never plan on stopping either, sometimes I can't believe this game is 9 years old and the graphics are this insane, like it's better on then some of the games that released last year, favorite game when it released and still favorite game to date. And played more then 800 hours still haven't 100% it lol

Absolute masterpiece from any perspective. Great storytelling, excellent  graphics (even hard to believe that switch can handle that), true open-world experience, deep gameplay mechanics which easily can make you fun for more than 100 hours. Must-play game for everyone.
My fav game oat. Crazy replayability, crazy gameplay, crazy level design, played more than 200 hours and never got bored. Thx to Nintendo for the work.

So yeah, I’ve probably spent way too many hours in Hyrule, but honestly, Breath of the Wild is just one of those games that you can’t ever really stop thinking about once you’ve experienced it. It’s not just a game, it’s like this weird, beautiful, lonely, and yet totally chaotic world that just lets you be whoever you want to be. I remember the first time I stepped out of the Shrine of Resurrection—that pan over the Great Plateau? Absolute cinema. It’s a masterclass in game design because it doesn’t give you a checklist or a map full of annoying icons. It just says, "Hey, see that mountain? You can climb it. See that weird glow in the distance? Go figure it out." And you do. You just wander. You get distracted by a butterfly, then a group of Bokoblins, then you find a shrine, and suddenly four hours have passed and you haven’t even touched the main quest. That kind of freedom is rare. Most games talk about being open world, but then they hit you with invisible walls or level-gating. BotW doesn't care about any of that. If you’re smart enough (or crazy enough), you can go straight to the final boss from the start. That’s just insane to me.I love **** let’s talk about the gear for a sec because, man, the items in this game are actually legendary once you figure out how to use them. I’m a huge horse guy in games, and the way they handled the horses here feels so grounded. But the real game-changer is when you get your hands on the Ancient Saddle and Bridle. Before those, your horse was kinda just... there. You’d leave it behind because you climbed a cliff and it couldn't follow you. But with that Ancient set? You whistle and boom, teleportation. It makes the bond with your horse feel so much more useful and, honestly, it adds to that cinematic feeling when you’re galloping through a field at night and you can just summon your ride whenever you need to make a quick getaway from a Guardian. It’s those little things, the quality of life stuff mixed with the "Chemistry Engine" where fire actually burns things and lightning is attracted to your metal shield, that makes the world feel like a real place rather than just a digital **** thing that really got to me, and I know I’m a total **** for this kind of stuff, is the whole tragic, romantic undertone of the story, especially involving Mipha. I’m a big fan of those cinematic, slightly heartbreaking backstories, and hers just hits differently. It’s not forced down your throat, it’s just there in the memories and the way the Zora people talk about her. The fact that she made that Zora Armor specifically for Link as a proposal... and then she died before she could even give it to him or tell him how she felt? That’s some heavy stuff. Every time I see her spirit on Vah Ruta, or use Mipha’s Grace to save my skin in a fight, it feels like this lingering, protective love that survived a hundred years of darkness. It gives the whole "save the world" thing a lot more weight because it’s not just about a kingdom, it’s about the people who loved you and aren't there anymore. It’s subtle, it’s romantic in a very sad way, and it makes the quiet moments of the game feel so much **** music, man... it's just perfect. Some people wanted a big, bombastic theme playing 24/7, but they’re wrong. The silence is what makes the music work. You're wandering through the ruins of a village, and you just hear these sparse, lonely piano notes that feel like they're mourning the world with you. But then, when things get real, the soundtrack absolutely **** final push toward Hyrule Castle is probably the most "epic" feeling I’ve ever had in a Zelda game. The way the music shifts from the outdoor theme to that heavy, orchestral march as you get closer to the sanctum is incredible. And that final fight with Calamity Ganon? It’s pure spectacle. When the Divine Beasts fire their lasers and the Champions' spirits are there with you, it feels like this massive culmination of everything you’ve suffered through. The second phase out on the field, with the sun low on the horizon and Zelda calling out to you while the music swells... that’s the kind of stuff I play games for. It’s grand, it’s emotional, and it feels like a true ending to a century-old tragedy. You’re not just hitting a boss; you’re ending a nightmare. It’s the perfect mix of gameplay and pure cinematic storytelling.Seriously, if you haven't played this yet, I don't know what to tell you. It’s a masterpiece. The weapons break, the rain makes you slide off cliffs, and you’ll probably die a hundred times to lightning or falling rocks, but that’s the charm. It’s a raw, beautiful experience that respects the player’s intelligence and emotions.I still go back just to ride my horse through the fields sometimes. It's just that good.

Breath of the Wild is one of the best games ever designed. Every aspect about it is amazing, and this isn't just blind praise. Coming from someone who has spent the last 6 years with this game, I can say with absolute confidence that you are missing out if you've never played this before. Get it now.
A pinnacle in Nintendo gaming that shows their capabilities for still creating top-level expansive adventure games with IP we grew up with. This game was my go-to during the pandemic
Solid AAA game! What you expect from Nintendo. Masterpiece on par with the best Zelda games

Magnum opus of Nintendo Switch games. Although the bosses are hard, making the game challenging makes the game fun and also it has multiple things to grind like getting the Master Sword, Koroks and a lot more, still top 5 Nintendo.
this was my first Nintendo Switch Game and its one of my favorite games of all time, the freedom, curiosity, nature, everything in this game its amazing
This The game is really important for me. The atmosphere, the story, the lore, everything in this game is incredible. This game really touched me.
A good game but i will always think its overrated as hell. Still an enjoyable experience though
This Zelda game is one of the best opuses. An endless open world, sliced in regions, each one being visually astonishing and full of interesting encounters. Could be the one, if there was not OoT...
The game that brought an entire new generation of open world games as well as and absurd amount of open world in Nintendo games LOL! One of the best games ever made!

The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild is not just a game, it is a revolution. A true masterpiece that redefines the very idea of the open world, it doesn’t simply invite exploration; it demands it. From the very first moments, the player is driven by pure curiosity, by the irresistible urge to wander, to climb, to look beyond the next hill just to see what lies there. Exploration is never forced or guided,it feels natural, organic, and deeply personal.What truly sets Breath of the Wild apart is its extraordinary artistic direction and attention to detail. Every landscape feels hand-crafted, every ruin tells a silent story, and every sunrise over Hyrule is a moment worth stopping for. The game’s physics system is nothing short of astonishing: fire spreads with the wind, metal conducts electricity, and the environment reacts intelligently to the player’s actions. This level of systemic depth makes experimentation endlessly rewarding and turns even simple encounters into memorable experiences.Most of all, Breath of the Wild is profoundly immersive. It creates a version of Hyrule that feels alive, vast, and breathtakingly beautiful,a world you never want to leave. Hours pass unnoticed as the game constantly fuels your desire to explore just a little more, to uncover one more secret, to lose yourself completely. It is a game that reshapes expectations, sets a new standard for open-world design, and remains unforgettable long after the console is turned off. Simply one of the best game of all time, or perhaps the greatest.

This game is really good. Like, really good. I'd need a full college-level essay to truly go in depth with explaining this game. Maybe I'll do that someday. The game is very intuitive, and from the second you get off the Great Plateau, the whole game is open to you. You can go on the recommended path, or you can go straight to Ganon. The world is filled with neat little things, and the mechanics are really diverse, especially the more complicated mechanics that are really rewarding when you pull them off. Overall, 9 out of 10. And I really wish I could experience the opening cinematic for the first time again.

The game has some appealing elements like the open world and familiar music, but overall it is quite frustrating. The gameplay is often repetitive and tedious, with many dull moments that drag the experience down. The story feels weak and uninspired, failing to keep the player engaged. Technical issues and clunky mechanics make it hard to recommend, resulting in a game that feels like a missed opportunity for the series.

I really have liked zelda games over the years but this one is just frustrating. The stamina meter is just a hassle. And who thought it was a gd idea for your weapons and shields to constantly break sooo annoying. Not to mention the horse is useless because it never is within range for your call. Frustrating game all the way around so disappointed.

Absolutely overrated the people making comparisons to the first Zelda game probably haven't played it there was a reason to explore and it felt rewarding to do so. Breath of the wild and its successor tears of the Kingdom are an ocean wide but puddle deep there is no real sense of progression in the game almost everywhere you go you just find rupees or arrows or some other useless gem. It's okay for an open World sandbox but it's garbage for a Zelda game and it has spat on classic fans of the series.

It's not a game that's worth throwing away, but it's not as great as Nintendo fans say it is. It's definitely not a game that will make you want to buy a console. Actually, no Nintendo game will make you want to buy a console. Maybe Splatoon and Metroid are okay, but the rest are complete garbage, like games for 3-year-olds. This device was created so that you can hand it to your crying child and let them play with this Italian **** who looks like a house painter. (When you say "console," it sounds cool, but it's just a step up from mobile games.) Even Sony CEO said these are babysitting tools, lol. (Sony games are aimed at 13-year-olds, tho. They play Detroit and get all worked up about the story, but they've never experienced well written story in their lives.)

-All the side quests are fetch quests. If Ubisoft made it, everyone would trash it, but since Nintendo made it, you give it a 95 score so the fans won't be upset. What's up with that, reviewers? The main quests are also bad, trash.
-The game is overly anime-like and childish. These are things that no self-respecting person over 20 should play.
-We weren't expecting a story, but it's still cliché and generic trash. The narrative is too open-ended and basic, pretending to be integrated into the gameplay. (Like Elden Ring)
-The map isn't a full-on theme park like Elden Ring or Immortals Fenyx Rising. It feels more natural at least, but it's still not a well-designed map. Those who rave about the best open world and exploration experience probably tried their first open-world games. Reviewers are really morons. This is mid-tier and nothing special or revolutionary in terms of open world or exploration.
-The voice acting and music are really bad. Combat is passable.
-The shrines are repetitive and not very appealing. Climbing towers and solving environmental puzzles is tedious. For example, tower climbing in Far Cry 4 was more enjoyable, but it was removed in subsequent games due to heavy criticism. Now, the same people are praising the same type of tower climbing and puzzle mechanics, but they're worse. What's with the hypocrisy, man? Despite all these negatives, BOTW is still a better game than RDR 2, Witcher 3, Elden Ring, and others. Still, the only reason it gets so much praise is because it's a Nintendo game. The so-called "clone" Immortals Fenyx Rising is actually 26,257 times better than this game and TOTK, but since it's an Ubisoft production, it's dismissed as a "clone" or "trash," denying objective reality. (BOTW and TOTK are actually minimalist Ubisoft formulas, but no one accuses them of copying Ubisoft?)

Unfortunately the game is too repetitive, bland, and empty with little enemy variety and poor gameplay decisions such as weapon durability. The world building is lackluster and the game is a slog to get through. The engine and world aesthetics are pretty nice, but it feels more like a demo compared to the better expansive open world games that exist.

I think most zelda games just don’t appeal to me. I’m not a big fan of the open world I think it makes the areas feel empty and lacking in content. I like the combat and the visuals but I never got hooked like most people did.
Overrated game, I didn't see any innovation in mechanics, story and graphics, I believe the high score is due to the nostalgia and fan club of the brand

BotW has some flaws, but you overlook some of them because BotW is an gameit's a great game. After all, it's one of a kind; however, you can't do that forever. You might be thinking, problems? What problems does this game have? Well. The map is too big and take too long to get to any destination, the character designs are fine, there's too many shrines, there's 900 korok seeds, all weapons break but that's fine because weapons are everywhere, the master sword the thing that is said to be the strongest sword and is meant to seal ganon has a LIMIT on how much it can be used before you have to recharge it. WHAT?!?! This sword is supposed to be all-powerful, but it seems kinda weak. Not counting the one-hit obliterator, the master sword isn't even the strongest weapon in the game, even though its lore it's supposed to be. Not to mention, it's also really tedious to get, and you have to go out of your way to even get it, which no other LoZ game even does. Another thing that's different from other Zelda games is that there's barely any LoZ lore or main stuff; it doesn't have Ganon. no calamity ganon is NOT ganon. If you removed the link from the game, you would NOT be able to tell that it's a LoZ game. This game barely feels like a Zelda game. I mean, it doesn't even have the Tri-Force. But you can excuse this by the fact that this is Nintendo's first attempt at an open-world Zelda. At least they'll fix all these problems in the next game. right? They'll fix all these problems, right?
Go through the whole game for there not to be a post game. Just loops you back to the final fight. Feel like I wasted my time with this in a way I haven't felt before.

I started again on Switch 2 and I don't know... The game feels so much worse than back than. It's a one-hit death parade. Very frustrating. It's harder at the early game than at the late game.Weird and bad game design. The graphics are low in contrast and full of fog. Milky and dusty screen. But a bit better than TOTK. And while there are no real dungeons and items inside, which can be use to have progress in the game, it feels very boring. I just wish the next game (after Totk) for Switch 2 will be much better. For me, playing is no longer fun at the moment. TOTK is a bit better in gameplay design, but lacks on similar things like missing progress like in legacy Zelda games. I like the open world and freedom in the game. But I'm sure that it is possible to combine the legacy with modern Zelda elements. We'll see...

As someone new to the Zelda franchise, I really tried to understand the hype, but after about 20 hours of gameplay, I honestly don’t see it. I picked up the game based on amazing reviews and high ratings, expecting an engaging open-world adventure filled with monsters, secrets, and exciting loot. At first, it seemed promising. But once I got into the core of the game, it quickly became underwhelming. The open world, while visually appealing, feels mostly empty. I was hoping for diverse areas packed with interesting content, but what I found instead were large stretches of space populated by the same handful of copy-pasted enemies. Exploration didn’t feel rewarding—it felt repetitive. Combat is decent. I can see why some players enjoy it. There’s a certain charm in the way battles play out, but the poor weapon durability system really drags it down.
Finished on switch 2. It was fun for awhile but after beat Gabon i think i am done with it

I can't in good faith rate a game that runs so poorly much higher. I know people say "oh it is just the hardware", but that should be a thing they had in mind when releasing the game. Drops to 15fps should be completely unacceptable.

At first I thought it’s a good Zelda game. But removing the fan glasses I see what a bad designed and unfair game it is. And the weird thing is, that the game is harder in early game than in late game. Means it’s nothing for new players. No dungeons, only repetitive boring stuff. Totk has more improvements than I thought. At this moment the game is no fun. I hope the next Zelda for Switch 2 is much better than this catastrophic.

The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild is an ambitious open-world game that impresses in some areas but falls short in others. The sense of exploration is its greatest strength, with a vast, beautiful world that encourages curiosity and discovery. Unfortunately, that world often feels empty, with repetitive shrines and copy-paste enemy encounters that wear thin over time. The story is minimal and lacks the emotional weight seen in other Zelda titles, leaving little incentive to push forward beyond pure exploration. Weapon durability adds unnecessary frustration rather than meaningful challenge. There are undeniable moments of wonder, but they come buried under a lot of filler. It is a good game, but far from the flawless masterpiece some make it out to be.
Mastersword **** in this title. It's not a legend of Zelda game anymore, it's boring.

The most overrated game of all time the open world is empty boring and tedious to roam around in there is literally nothing to do im gonna DIE of boredom atleast with a bad game i can have some fun and laugh at it for being so bad but this game just has nothing to do

A horribly overrated Japanese game. This game lacks enemy variety. What you do is explore Hyrule and solve puzzles in random temples. One of the more annoying parts is the constant weapon breaking. It doesn't matter if you get the master sword, as that too breaks after a certain number of hits, the exception being that it will be re-added to your inventory. Weapon-breaking is added to many other games like The Souls series, but those are way less annoying. There's no way you can unlock the full story without reading a walkthrough. If you like a weapon more than the master sword, you cannot stick to using it over and over, as those inevitably break. Most bosses also don't pose 
a serious challenge to you, as they are just gimmick bosses who throw more puzzles at you.
I don't really know why people like this so much. I wish I enjoyed it but it just feels... pointless?

A definite departure from the traditional Zelda games with open world elements that are reminiscent of of say WoW or similar games. I miss the older styles of dungeons as the dungeons in this game really don’t have a lot of challenge. What does get very challenging is the end game gameplay near the castle. Almost too challenging compared to the rest of the game.

I’m sorry, but I’ve restarted the game four times at different points in my life, and I’ve never been able to enjoy it. I don’t think I’ll ever try TOTK either. I just feel lost, like the world is empty, and I couldn’t even finish the game because I had no idea where to go or what to do. I think I completely missed the point of the game, but four attempts in a row is a lot, so I’m pretty certain,I just don’t like BOTW.

It always amazes me how people can have such widely differing views on pretty much any topic. This game is no different. While most reviews are overwhelminly positive about Breath of the Wild, I found it to be tedious and mind numbingly boring. I don't get how people can really like it. This is the first Zelda game (and I've played almost all of them) that I really disliked. It's beautiful, to be sure, but empty of any real substance which makes a game interesting for me. To each their own, I suppose.
a huge world of busywork, every boss the same, story just boring. worst zelda period.

34/100
Absolutely underwhelming! In times of The Witcher and other much better open world games, this game was rated quite positively at the time, which I find totally incomprehensible. The gameplay is marred by completely unnecessary elements: a few hits break a weapon, really bad graphics (actually important in an open world with a wide view so that the world can have an impact), massive stuttering on the in-house console, the story is rather irrelevant, Krog seeds are just boring. Only the temples, which are also neglected, were interesting.

Grades on MC suggest you should get ready for absolutely top of what gaming has to offer. Unfortunately this game is more of a relict of the past that Nintendo cannot and is not able to leave behind.
No dubbing (that one there is only stresses out that full dubbing is missing),practically non-existent story, repetitive gameplay and assets. 
Had this game been cut into pieces, you would not feel you have missed anything.

I do not buy this open world concept. I adored LA Noire, where open world was only a stage for the story, but completely empty map with some redundant collectibles does not make this game great.

Game feeds on lowest gamer instinct of collecting and exploring but is shallow and too repetitive to be something truly unforgetable.

Great starting title for Switch. Absolutely average game all in all. If it was not called Zelda it would have a rating closer to 7. This is the true power of Nintendo, promoting average games into "legends"
Interesting world with interesting plot, but the graphics and style are unique (subjectively, but in my opinion in a bad way)

The beginning of the end and the start of Ubislop "Nu-Zelda".

Avoid this festering diseased sore like the plague.

My 4th Zelda game & 3rd one I couldn't bare to finish, I just don't get the appeal, even with this one being different & a true open world yada yada, I'd rather just play Genshin Impact than this, lol. Found nothing about this game interesting, Link himself I've never been liked, I tried to give this game a chance & played for several hours & found none of it even a little fun.

Breath of the Wild is a great game, no doubt, however, it's far from a great Zelda game. The characters are amazingly designed, and their segments are amazing. My favorite part of this game was learning about the champions, Mipha, Daruk, Urbosa, and Revali, as well as the champion descendants, Sidon, Teba, Yunobo, and Riju. Learning about them was easily my favorite part about the game. Yes, areas like Zora's Domain and Rito Village were fun to explore but nothing was really there for the Longterm it was all esthetic beauty. Breath of the Wild in my opinion was weighed down by the Zelda franchises name.
It's a really amazing game, but to be honest I just never liked this open world style of Zelda. I really wish they could make another Zelda 3D in the future.
Don't get me wrong, this game is good, but it's simply not worth, Grab this when it's on sale.
Those who made this Game should be ashamed of themselves. As if they never played a Zelda Game in their Life

It's anathema to hate a Zelda game when Ocarina of Time is your favorite game ever, yet here we are.   I hate even 'minor' details, like hidden collectible Korok seeds being found on the rooves of peoples makeshift shelters, forcing Link to run around on top of their bivouacs like a jackass.  It's gonna leak like the titanic in WindWaker the next time it rains.   Imagine finding someone's simplistic wooden shelter in the forest and then jogging around on top of it. Only a complete moron would hide a collectible in such a location.   The combat bored me and I hated the new weapon durability system.  You don't have enough inventory space to carry all the weapons you find, and EVERYTHING breaks in a handful of swings.   You're back in the inventory system interrupting the gameplay over and over and over again switching to your next weapon.    The cooking system is another massive unnecessary time sink unbefitting a hero of time.   Can't the millions of other civilians who never help you fight do your cooking for you?    Inventory management simulator.      The fun of cooking is people TASTING your food afterwards, but it's a videogame and VR is nowhere near that realistic yet.    The whole thing feels like unpaid work, like clocking into a job you hate and never receiving a paycheck at the end of the month.   I can't think of anything positive to say.   It rides on the coattails of open-world masterpieces like Skyrim, managing to be both WAYYY late to the party and WORSE in every conceivable way, including graphically.    This "Open Air" game is so late to the scene that it's almost nonsensical to compare it to all the other masterpiece open world pioneers of the genre.    GTA IV, Skyrim, Far Cry 2,3, Assassin's Creed 1, 2.  Borderlands, World of Warcraft, ETC, ETC, ETC, are all vastly superior looking and playing games that came out years before. BOTW brings nothing new to the table that could be misconstrued as innovative - All it's new inventory management time wasting ideas feel like a drag on the entire genre.   Ironically, the game begins with Link waking up from cryosleep having slumbered through the past 100 years, which is essentially what this game did, sleep through a decade of dozens of superior masterpieces that released in the intervening years. The art style is simply a WindWaker knock-off without the cel-shaded cuteness, both less impressive from an originality perspective and an artistic one.   Technically it may be pushing higher resolutions and polygon counts, yet it still manages to look worse than the 2003 GameCube game, which is **** insane.   I hate the Switch controller, both the num-chuk  joy-cons (horrendous) and the pro controller, which is merely a 2005 Xbox 360 controller that's far less ergonomic and yet somehow costs more money : the buttons are sticky and the triggers ****, plus it's uncomfortable in the hands - as both were designed to be held by 6 year olds.   The score is forgettable, so much so that I completely forgot it even had music until I had to sit down and write this review.   Not awful, but so rote that the game might as well not have been scored at all.   Seriously, they could have left it out of the game entirely and recorded some bird calls and some wind rustling through the trees and crammed it in here instead and saved the money.   Why was Koji Kondo not in charge of the music this time around?   Blow me, Nintendo.    Having no music probably would have been more effective anyway, I don't know why they even bothered.  Listen to the sounds of nature as you navigate a vast open world. Makes sense to me.  The music would not have been missed -  how am I even saying this about a Zelda game for Christ's sake?   Plot wise it's an even bigger disaster :   It's a complete rip-off of Wind Waker, just like the graphics.    An apocalyptic event has taken place and left Hyrule Castle inaccessible to the player.   Civilization has had to rebuild from the ruins and create a new world over 100 years.   Instead of the old world being drowned by and endless torrential downfall of rain, leaving only the mountain peaks as islands,  Calamity Ganon is trapped inside Hyrule Castle instead of it being trapped under the ocean.   It's literally the exact same plot as WW.   The cryo-sleep **** instead of being the ancestor of Link.    It's the same exact **** thing.   Instead of entering and cleansing temples, you enter and cleanse the giant magic beasts with their **** graphics and ugly architecture.   They're the **** ugliest and least thematic temples in Zelda history.    Ask any Zelda player to recall their favorite temples from the franchise.    The colors, the music, the atmosphere...  in this game, all the temples are the innards of giant **** robots.   You'd have to be **** to come up with such a stupid idea.    Exploring the insides of giant stupid **** robots.  That's the theme of ALL the temples.  Robots with the same textures reused again and again.
the legend of zelda thats wild makes y6ou fell like spider men and i cannot stress enoughow nig thjis world islke wow i gues mimntendo kockedliek wiow 7 out of ten because im i gn

Take a successful open world formula perfected in games like Horizon:Zero Dawn and The Witcher;  add in mechanics making your weapons break, a weight burden system,  and mountains impossible to traverse and you have The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild.  While a huge step forward for Nintendo, there are better versions of this type of game to be had.  
The graphics are a nice leap forward, especially when the Switch is in handheld mode.  But, the negatives of the game far outweigh the positives.
There's nothing to say. Boring games, boring battles and enemies.Very low-level picture

I also had tons of fun with the special abilities such as magnesis and whatnot. Exploration is a lot of fun on the first and maybe even second time through

It lacks replay ability, the story and characters are just ok, and there's honestly not enough content variety. The enemies and collectibles in this game are highly repetitive, and there should have been more fully fleshed questlines. The dungeons in this game are probably the worst in the entire series.

It's pretty good. I recommend at least one playthrough, as it has widespread appeal

I personally think this game is great but at the same time has a lot of problems, for one the world feels empty which i know is the point because of the story but it just doesn't work for me, the bosses also felt a little bland. And lastly, if they expanded on the story at least a little bit even though they do but in a different way i think it would all come full circle.

First f**k critics reviews, they seem to be all buyed out.
I tried giving it a chance and played the whole game (all main quests and some side-quests), spent more than 50 hours with it but the game is so bland compared to previous Zelda games, it's so boring that I don't feel like playing Totk.

If they were to be more active on the way they delivered the story, and not the lazy "grab those random **** to learn about" the game would be much better.

Hard to believe that it was "better" than Skyward and Twilight.

The first line in the Metacritic summary says it all: "Ignore everything you know about The Legend of Zelda games.". Quite. And "Breath of the Wild" is *not* a Legend of Zelda game.

Legend of Zelda games have always centred themselves around a traditional story, an astonishing variety of gameplay (with each new dungeon comes a whole new experience), and a vast roll-call of quirky, sweetly funny NPCs (in a word, *personality*).

None of these are present in Breath of the Wild. It's hard to say which of these failings contributes most heavily to the disaster that is this tedious, stolid lump ****; I rank them all first equal. 

There is *no story* to speak of. No sense of  "I must do this, because these characters, or these lands as a whole, are depending upon me". I'll say it again to let it sink in. This is a (nominally) a *Zelda* game with *no story* to speak of. What were they thinking?

There is very little variety in the gameplay. After a couple of hours or so, you've more or less done everything you'll be doing in this game. There are no dungeons (or other locations which present a sustained challenge). There are only a handful of bosses, most of which are variations on the same uninteresting mechanic. You'll spend more than half your time either doing micro-mini "challenge" rooms (the tiny self- contained, largely repetitive puzzle rooms which appear to be the game's excuse for there being no dungeons or memorable locations), or taking down enemy camps (which feature a quite astonishingly small roster of enemy types, all dull, all defending nothing in particular). Neither of these activities earn you anything new, except crafting materials. Crafting was mercifully absent from Zelda games until now, and now it is basically the whole game. Collecting stuff for crafting weapons and armour all of which break after a short while - that's the centre of the BotW experience.

And thirdly the game is pretty much devoid of personality (and personalities). Very little of the sweet humour, very little in the way of the weird and wonderful characters of varying species which have always been the much needed counterpoint to the seriousness of the main story. I guess they thought that since there is no serious story to speak of, why bother with the quirky counterpoint to it? There is really  only one village, which for some reason they inserted dozens of practically indistinguishable copies of all over the map. Each minor variant of this "village" (it's basically a tent) has as little of interest to do as the last one, and very few of the inhabitants have anything worthwhile to listen to. If you're lucky, there may be a fetch quest attached to this particular tent but no more than a tedious vanilla fetch or set of fetches.

Are there any positives? The world *does* have a rather gorgeous shimmery painted feel to it. In fact, they've managed to out-do the previous Zelda games which sought to do the same (Windwaker, Twilight Princess, Skyward Sword). And the fact that you can travel to any outdoor location you can see (and you can see admirably far) without any loading break is a real improvement over previous Zelda games, but this is nothing special nowadays (we expect this technical feat of every modern RPG).

That's really *it* for the plusses. In summary, what you have is a rather gorgeous overworld, into which a gorgeous Zelda game could have been inserted, had they been bothered. But this really *isn't* that Zelda game. It isn't really a Zelda game, period. The nearest comparison I can think of would be "Death Stranding"; another game set in a gorgeous overworld, with almost nothing at all interesting to do in that world, and with no feeling or heart to it.

Other people seem to like it. I don't care. I quite genuinely cannot understand what they see in it, and it's quite hard to stomach the notion that anyone giving this game a glowing review is actually a fan of the Zelda series. Nothing that fans of the Zelda series love in those games is on display here.

Honestly, I do not understand the hype other than **** game design is pretty subpar for the positive feedback it gets. The open world is not that interesting to explore, there is no guidance whatsoever, the side missions are pretty lame, and here are some absolutely ???? feelings:1. No weapon repair. You can’t even see how much life it has until it will break soon. How come you cannot upgrade weapons or armor? This makes this game so much more limiting, and I cannot beat certain enemies unless I…wander aimlessly perhaps FOREVER until I find a good enough weapon and armor? And even then it still isn’t enough so I keep putting this game down for periods of time because I don’t feel motivated to keep trying.2. No leveling up. This is literally such a basic part of game design that would at least motivate me to fight enemies. I have no incentive which makes me just avoid them altogether. Their weapon drops and items are absolutely not worth losing precious weapons and arrows over.3. No real story. It’s just a lot of memories that don’t progress a plot (this is lazy storytelling). A narrative built on exposition is not a narrative. It is exposition. And exposition is pointless if it doesn’t support a narrative. Yawn.Good design motivates players to play, rewards them for putting in extra time and effort, gives them opportunities/options to progress in the story or keep doing side missions to explore and get more skill and items, and has a good narrative to be a strong foundation for gameplay. This game fails to do any of this.I would not buy this game or recommend this game, let alone spend a lot of time on it. It is more frustrating and time consuming than it is enjoyable and rewarding which is, like…the point of games…Genuinely wish I could understand why people claim this game to be so superior. I review this game based on every other game, not just previous Zelda games made in a different era of technology. In today’s age of video games, this one is not worth much. But if you are obsessed with Zelda, go right ahead.

Game ran sub 20 fps, unacceptable for a game released post 2005. What little I saw of the story was unengaging and the gameplay itself felt like an assassins creed/Skyrim remix instead of a zelda game. Its literally the only zelda game I got bored of and quit less than an hour before the tutorial was over.

Zelda: Breath of the Wild is for a certain type of person, and that person is not me. I could not disagree more with the mainstream consensus on this game and franchise. I loved Majora's Mask and thought Ocarina of Time was pretty good. Most of the gameplay loop in Breath of the Wild was boring and slow. The weapons breaking over time is frustrating, the powers are just okay, the combat is slow, and the art style is fine if you like that sort of thing, but it's not for me.

A step forwards in Zelda exploration, but a step backwards in story. I enjoyed the combat, especially Lynels. Dungeons are somewhat disappointing, and gameplay can get very repetitive. Not the best game I've ever played, but certainly one that is worthwhile.

Pretty good, really like how you could choose the order you complete the main story and the characters were really well done. My gripes are the weapon durability system made me not ever want to use the good weapons because they essentially have the same durability as a twig, which isn't fun. Let me use what weapons I want without having to feel punished for it. Also after a while the world just felt empty. The little sidequests here and there were fun but after a while I didn't wanna solve some convoluted ass puzzle for one cheap useless gem. Some of the side quests were more confusing than Elden Ring's side quests, and those have absolutely no direction at all. There wasn't much actual incentive to explore. The shrines were fun, I never got tired of them. Definitely a good game, and they did a lot of unique stuff that no game has other done, but in my opinion this game is the definition of "overrated". Good but not amazing like everyone makes it out to be

These new Zeldas arent Zelda games. They are generic open world RPGs with a Zelda skin slapped on. The weapon breaking systems are terrible. There is no iconic characters or music the way there was in the older titles. Its just so watered down and unfortunately glazed to oblivion.

I wanted to like this game more. I really, really did. Breath of the Wild has of the best aesthetics, some of the most charming characters, and maybe the most impressive vision I’ve ever seen for a game… but these positives don’t make up for the game’s sins. Especially the cardinal sin of games: Breath of the Wild is a *boring* video game.

- A needlessly frustrating weapon-breaking system designed to “encourage versatility” that only serves to force players into certain behaviors in combat (in stark contrast to the game’s approach to exploration, where fine-tuned physics really do encourage and reward creativity in a way seldom done better). Good weapons essentially become the Ethers of BotW, rare resources which you stockpile in your inventory and never use, because nothing seems important enough to risk your most precious gear on, even in the final climb up Hyrule Castle. You will never, ever convince me that a knight’s sword **** into a thousand pieces after twenty-something swings is either realistic OR fun.

- The physics-based gameplay, which an absolute revelation for exploration, makes most puzzles meaningless. There are clear intended solutions for most brain-teasers scattered across the game, as the puzzles in Breath of the Wild are kept fairly simple by the franchise’s standards, but it’s equally easy in most cases to disregard what the game wants you to do in favor of just breaking the puzzle by brute-forcing your way to the reward using the game’s physics. Figuring out the solution to a particularly tough Zelda puzzle and getting over that mound and progressing after pounding your head against the game again and again is one of the most rewarding experiences in the gaming canon, and Breath of the Wild just doesn’t have that. 

- A vast, expansive and immersive open world with not nearly enough stuff to do in it. Fitting to the post-apocalyptic setting of this new Hyrule, sure, but that doesn’t make those long stretches of empty travel time any less boring. Putting nine hundred Koroks across your world (most of which are hidden behind those brute-forceable physics puzzles, might I add) doesn’t make it an engaging environment. Empty worlds *can* be done in an interesting and engaging way—Fallout built an entire franchise around this idea—but this reimagining of Hyrule is simply too quiet.

- An outright disregard for the established source material and timeline. I *really* want to like this game’s story, because a lot of it is just so well-written, and I understand that the Zelda timeline can be confusing and tricky to write around. But choosing to cherry pick names and concepts while throwing out decades of established continuity in favor of something “fresh” is like taking clippings of your baby’s hair before throwing them out with the bath water. It was, at least to my way of thinking, the wrong move.

I’ve played this game front-to-back, and I’ve sunk my teeth more deeply into the lore than I care to admit. And my biggest takeaway from this game is… I’m really, really gonna miss classic Zelda now that it’s gone.

Personally, I say I'm giving Breath of the Wild the benefit of the doubt it would be a 2 or a 3 if this wasnt a great game. Honestly I'd consider this a 9 or a 10 if it didn't pretend to be Zelda. 

In my opinion, one of, if not Zelda's lowest point. And I've been an avid Zelda fan since Ocarina of time and Majora's Mask. Also, no this is not just nostalgia calling. I have played Breath of the Wild more than four times now and I still haven't been able to capture what I felt when I first played it.

Breath of the Wild is fun for a first time playthrough but the open world aspect dies out the moment you explore each corner of the map and realize it's just another Korok or just another shrine. The Divine Beast boss battles were fun and the Blight Ganons were different. And heck even I liked the DLC but those things don't make a Zelda experience, do they?

And look I'm all down for customizable link and open world and all that. But come on if your going to plaster Zelda's name on it at least make it feel somewhat like Zelda. This new format has something, but it needs to be seriously refined.
I honestly don't find this game to be fun. The controls are horrible and the combat plus puzzles are not better than other games

I mean it’s a decent time, but temper your expectations if you go into this believing the GOTY hype. The land is unique although bland and repetitive, the story is fine, the combat is solid.  This is a game that should be played, but nowhere near living up to the hype.

Let me be clear right now: I HAVE NOT FINISHED THIS GAME nor am I ANYWHERE CLOSE! 

I don't like Zelda breath of the wild and I don't think it's the game's fault. Zelda BOTW is definitely one of the best open world games ever made, it's just not my thing. I don't like open world games and I think that was made very clear when I played this game. I found it boring and not fun at all. Maybe it gets better as the game progresses, I don't know. The beginning of my dislike for the series. I prefer open worlds but the lack of story, game design, annoying bird, champions replacing sages? TOTK kind of tried to reintroduce sages but ultimately failed... I won't be returning to this series if the game after Tears of the Kingdom doesn't fix up

As Zack Says in FF7 series: the price of freedom is steep. i definitely enjoyed this game and think it does do alot with open world revitalization and is good, but boy it sacrifices what i love about Zelda to do so. The world while vast and explorable is very desolate despite being explained there are huge sections of emptiness. The characters are fine but with the story based on past and memories lose impact of ones like midna. While the memories are fine and a smart approach imo, they end up scattered and tell a just okay story in comparison to other games. The rewards for exploration feel poor: broken parts not helpful armor, stuff that has too much single use leaving behind excellent dungeons items. Speaking of which, the dungeons are gone, leaving non interesting repeat shrines, or mediocre divine beasts in their wake which is a bummer. This game is lauded for design yet it has 10 enemy types or so which are so repeat or just nothing, and the combat is the same as ever. sure it has fun you can do but there isn't incentive to approaching stuff differently. I feel this game repeats the same fox following tower climbing open world deign but never gets the flak for it and does the same as others before it yet gets praised, because... Nintendo? The special powers are fine, and the game is... fine. I don't see the same revolution and it loses Zelda's dungeon crawling, lore and story plus appeal for fans of what came before man it does not feel great. Kinda overrated.
It’s a beautiful game with a lot of depth and a wide variety of ways to approach the game. It’s also quite boring in my opinion. To each their own.

Nothing short of overrated. As a lifelong Zelda fan, I had high hopes for this "revolutionary" game, yet as I progressed through the game looking for clues as to why the general public continually endorses this game, all I seemed to find was disappointment. 

Not only is the story minimal, it is mostly locked behind "lost memories" scattered across the map, which wouldn't be that bad if it weren't for the fact it doesn't add anything of value to the divine beast flashbacks or the intro, effectively making the whole ordeal of chasing these memories down completely and utterly useless.

On to the so-called 'incredible' combat, you have the choice between 3 different weapons varying in styles allowing for additional damage and/or durability, and that's pretty much it. Hit, hit, and block makes for boring combat against overleved monsters that drop useless loot, making fighting unsatisfyingly pointless, unless you're a fan of breaking your weapons for no reason (oh and they do break fast).

Puzzles are a recurrent element in Zelda games and most certainly make a comeback in Breath of the Wild in the form of Divine Beasts and numerous shrines. The beasts felt tedious, all over the place, and more so frustrating than challenging. The shrines on the other hand often felt too easy, a clear imbalance. 

As far as the overworld goes, this is a complete disaster. While the game looks promising at first, it soon comes to light upon further inspection that it is completely bland and devoid of life. Clearly aiming for quantity over quality, the vast green lands are clearly just here to limit test the Switch. For the game's purposes? Clearly no power and no point. 

On top of that, the game reeks of confusing and hidden elements. As someone who's played his fair share of videogames, my gamesense is developed enough for me to get a grasp **** basic features in a quick manner. However, BOTW hides gameplay elements so far behind walkthrough guides, I finished the game skipping over a ton of elements (none that would have enhanced my experience however). Not to mention, the iconic master sword and hylian shield are not even major story elements (if even minor ones) this time around so have fun with you bokoblin club I suppose.

TL;DR: In no way is this game revolutionary when other clearly superior games of the same genre exist. Clearly a proof of concept for the sequel, TOTK, or a game targeting kids (green and inexperienced).  Not worth the time nor the hype.

SKIP

This game was one of my biggest disapointments in gaming history. The story is mid, and the way they decided to manage it was not good. The exploration is the best part of the game, but even that gets repetitive after a few hours into it.

I really don't understand the hype about this game...  I'm a huge Zelda fan, I have LoZ tattoos... And it took me years and multiple tries to even get through this game.  Every time I tried, it slid off me like greased goose ****.
For reference, my favorite LoZ game is Ocarina of Time, but Link to the Past will always have a special place in my heard.  Twilight Princess was fun and I like some of the art choices.  Even Wind Waker was great, though repetitive and slow in places... but recent games, starting with Skyward Sword just feel dull to me.
Breath of the Wild combined two of my least favorite things you could do to a LoZ game.  
First, it eliminated the dungeons, and dungeon bosses that are perhaps some of the most iconic pieces of Zelda games.  I don't understand how someone could make that decision.  The puzzle shrines were nice, I guess... Maybe sick a dozen of them together, focusing on different ways to use a new item/ability and theme it around a particular terrain motif, culminating in a boss fight?
Second, the destructible weapons/shields felt jarring to me.  Zelda has always been a series about gaining powers, items, ect, on your quest.  Turning into a veritable Swiss Army Knife of abilities to solve puzzle, with combat being another puzzle to solve.  The idea that you could find something cool and powerful, but have the game rob you of it once you use it a couple times feels like something out of a different game.
The world and art are beautiful, and returning to the open world exploration roots of Zelda could be very interesting, but the story was so thin, and the challenges presented were so generic and formless that I felt like I was grinding through the game to get it over with, rather than going on a journey in a new take on a beloved world.
Also, the voice acting was terrible, particularly for Zelda...
Shallow, empty world. Mediocre graphics. There's no rush nor reason to complete any of the quests. Mechanics aren't great. Might've been great on the Wii U, but definitely overrated on the switch.

Probably the 4rth worst Zelda game out there.   Too much time walking around. I never felt challenged by any shrines. And all the worst temples are in that game.  Beautiful game. Fun mechanics, but way too easy.  MaybeI wouldhave loved this game if I didn't play all the past Zelda games.

I respect how much other people love this game and the time that they've put into adapting the game to their playstyle, but this game is not for me. In my opinion, there's too many systems in play here - you have an extensive quest log to manage, crafting materials to acquire, cooking recipes to learn and implement, gear to constantly manage and replace, pictures to investigate, etc etc etc. I respect that that's how some people want to play the game, but I wanted something more *focused*. With that said, I wasn't super keen on the "go anywhere and find your adventure" sort of game direction - I walked into one area and was instantly killed by lightning. I walked into another area and came face-to-face with a super powerful boss. The constant "die and try again" may be appealing to some, but not to me, especially when done for dozens of hours on end.
bad. completely overrated. the game has bad controls and a severe lack of replay ability.

Ah, where do I even begin with The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild? It's like trying to juggle flamingos while riding a unicycle made of bubblegum, all the while reciting Shakespeare backwards in a room filled with upside-down pineapples. This game is a surreal masterpiece, a cacophony of pixelated dreams woven together with threads of cosmic spaghetti. Playing it is akin to sipping moonlight from a teacup made of **** while tap-dancing on the rings of Saturn. The open world is as vast as the universe itself, filled with more mysteries than a riddle wrapped in an enigma and deep-fried in perplexity.Combat? It's like engaging in a sword fight with a rubber chicken while wearing socks made of marshmallows. The enemies, oh, the enemies! They're as diverse as a collection of mismatched socks in a laundromat run by interdimensional goblins. And the storyline? It's as convoluted as a labyrinth designed by a sentient crossword puzzle. You'll find yourself questioning the very fabric of reality, pondering the meaning of life, the universe, and why chickens always seem to have a bone to pick with you.Comparing this game to other titles is like comparing a supernova to a jellybean, or a symphony to a kazoo solo performed by a sloth. In the grand tapestry of gaming, Breath of the Wild is a neon-colored giraffe in a sea of monochrome ****, my dear readers, I bestow upon this masterpiece a solid 9 out of 10. Why not a perfect 10, you ask? Well, perfection is overrated, like trying to catch moonbeams in a fishing net or explaining the concept of time to a caffeinated squirrel. Embrace the chaos, revel in the absurdity, and let Link guide you through this wonderfully bewildering adventure.

Buying a Switch for this game is one of the greatest regrets I have in gaming. Visuals held back by outdated hardware, barebones story and extremely simple and generic gameplay make for one of the biggest disappointments in the time I spent gaming.
Nintendo copy paste again. 0 originality. Boring and from 2 generations in the past. Please innovate a bit, enough to cheat poor Nintendo users.
**** game. Very overhyped japan arcade with bad visual, lack of dialogs and content

This game follows the generic Nintendo game franchise called Zelda. In which the protagonist saves the princess. Want a more Nintendo “story” than this? He may be a plumber, a swordsman, but that's what it's all about.

The open world is completely empty of content, with repetitive puzzles called Shrine, and a story that makes any child sleepy.

The gameplay is 100% limited to Roll and Hit, typical of generic and industrial Souls Likes games, which are facilitated for players limited to pressing two buttons. The difference here is that we have more colors.

The game copied several mechanics from other games like Ark Online, among others, but for an outdated platform that is the Switch, this can be innovative.

I'm amazed how Nintendo manages to sell the same game for 30 years with the same story (Spoiler: no story) every time. Who knows, maybe next time they'll be able to innovate and make Link not lose his memory again.

I like the combat enough, some great music tracks here and there, and the overall atmosphere itself is definitely where the game shines. The exploration is fun but the world feels pretty empty at times, as well as the Switch's limited graphics can make things look a little lacklustre. The story felt pretty barebones, none of the characters were particularly interesting. The dungeons were very disappointing compared to what the Zelda series has put out in the past with the temples and other dungeon-like locations. As someone who plays mostly RPGs the durability system really didn't work for me, felt very arcadey. I get why people like it, but it didn't do enough for me.

Not my type of game. The building feels very gimmicky. Too cartoonish which is a missed opportunity with today's graphic potential.
Feels very much like Genshin Impact, though does almost every aspect poorly.
Empty world, bad graphics, non-existent story, temples with underwhelming rewards, extremely overrated.
A poor excuse for a Zelda game. No depth, lackluster characters, and not memorable overall. This and Totk are the Disney star wars movies of Zelda games.

Im returning my switch bc of this garbage ****. It looks and feels like a hobbyist made it and someone slapped a zelda logo on top. Replace the logo and the chars with no names and you get what this game actually is. A wasteland made of basically nothing

Open World: The Game
This game is simply dreadful and encompasses all the things I hate about open world games, while adding a few other game mechanics that only make the game worse. The game started out okay but once I got out of the tutorial area things went downhill quickly. Any attempts to further progress the story was met with a swift end by any number of enemies that will one shot you and you will randomly encounter these enemies just trying to get from point A to point B. Getting more hearts and stamina in this game is a chore. You have to complete a trial which you have to find first just out in the wild with no real easy way to locate them. Then you have to complete 4 of them to get 1 more heart or a minimal increase to links stamina wheel. I lost my patience doing these trials really quickly. Link needs stamina to pretty much do anything running, swimming and climbing all consume stamina and Link has so very little of it that trying to get anywhere takes so long that I would just rather turn the game off. There is cooking in this game because of course. Cooking is also an activity that takes forever. You first have to find a cooking pot and ingredients again randomly out in the wild. You then have to navigate some menus before adding ingredients one at a time to links hands before tossing the ingredients in the cooking pot which the ingredients will dance around for a bit before you get the meal you cooked. The meal you cook typically gives you health or stamina and thats pretty much it. It all just wastes your time everything in this game is just a waste of your time. There is a weapon durability system that is awful makes me not even really want to fight anything. The only thing enemies drop are monster parts that can be used in cooking to make elixirs that give you buffs. No recovery hearts. No rupees. No anything that I play The Legend of Zelda games for. There are certain areas that you cant go to without appropriate clothing or food buffs because the areas are to hot or to cold. Just a very unfun game. I tried doing some sidequests but the only rewards I got were weapons and shields. Since these shatter like glass anytime I try to use them I just didnt bother anymore. Returned the game for a refund after playing for roughly 8 hours and the only reason I'm not giving the game a 0/10 is because the game works and the graphics are actually pretty nice. Everything else about Breath of the Wild is terrible.

I promise I’ve tried and tried because of the great reviews. But not my kind of game. I don’t find it engaging or interesting. The quests and temples were tedious. I wish I could enjoy it as much as most people.
